---
title: "Government & Public Finances"
description: "Government debt and budget balance — Canada and OECD peers"
---

What governments owe, and whether they run surpluses or deficits, shapes the room
they have to act — on health care, infrastructure, and the interest bill itself.
These charts compare Canada's general-government finances with 17 OECD peers,
using the OECD Economic Outlook. The most recent year or two are projections
rather than final figures.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import os
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from pipeline.config import get_data_date, CATEGORICAL_COLORS, CATEGORICAL_OTHER
from pipeline.charts import peer_comparison_line, ranked_bar, ranked_bar_select
```

## Where Canada stands

A scorecard of where Canada ranks among the 17 peers on each measure (latest available year). Dots are ordered by rank, so **further right is more favourable for Canada** (lower-is-better measures are flipped accordingly). Canada is red, key comparators in colour; hover a dot for its value and rank.

```{python}
#| echo: false
from pipeline.charts import page_snapshot
page_snapshot("fiscal").show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
```

## Government gross debt

General government gross financial liabilities as a percentage of GDP — the broad measure of what all levels of government owe. This gross figure is higher than the "net" debt often cited in Canadian budgets, which subtracts financial assets the government holds.

```{python}
#| echo: false
debt_path = "data/fiscal/oecd_govt_debt.csv"
if os.path.exists(debt_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(debt_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "govt_debt",
        title="", yaxis_title="Gross debt (% of GDP)", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(debt_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Government debt data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest government debt: ranked

Use the dropdown to switch between two measures. **Gross debt** is the broad measure of what all levels of government owe. **Net debt** subtracts the financial assets governments hold (such as public-pension reserves); on this measure Canada's general-government debt is far lower than its gross figure and among the lowest in the peer group — a different picture from the gross-debt and deficit headlines. (Norway is omitted from the net measure: its sovereign wealth fund puts net debt at roughly −340% of GDP, far off this scale.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
netdebt_path = "data/fiscal/oecd_net_debt.csv"
if os.path.exists(debt_path) and os.path.exists(netdebt_path):
    gross = pd.read_csv(debt_path)
    nd = pd.read_csv(netdebt_path)
    nd = nd[nd["country_code"] != "NOR"]  # sovereign-wealth-fund outlier, off-scale on net
    fig = ranked_bar_select(
        [dict(label="Gross debt", df=gross, value_col="govt_debt",
              xaxis_title="Gross debt (% of GDP)",
              hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f}%<extra></extra>"),
         dict(label="Net debt", df=nd, value_col="net_debt",
              xaxis_title="Net debt (% of GDP)",
              hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.0f}%<extra></extra>")],
        f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(debt_path)}")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
else:
    print("Government debt data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Government budget balance

General government net lending as a percentage of GDP. Positive values are surpluses; negative values are deficits. The dotted line marks a balanced budget (zero).

```{python}
#| echo: false
bal_path = "data/fiscal/oecd_govt_balance.csv"
if os.path.exists(bal_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(bal_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "govt_balance",
        title="", yaxis_title="Budget balance (% of GDP)", show_average=True)
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_dash="dot", line_color="#888", opacity=0.5)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(bal_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34,
        showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Government balance data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest government budget balance: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(bal_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(bal_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "govt_balance", "Budget balance (% of GDP, surplus + / deficit −)",
        f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook | Data as of: {get_data_date(bal_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}%<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Government revenue

Total general-government revenue as a share of GDP — taxes plus other receipts, across all levels of government. It is the broadest measure of the size of government's claim on the economy.

```{python}
#| echo: false
rev_path = "data/fiscal/oecd_govt_revenue.csv"
if os.path.exists(rev_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(rev_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "govt_revenue", title="",
        yaxis_title="% of GDP", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(rev_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Government revenue data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest government revenue: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(rev_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(rev_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "govt_revenue", "% of GDP",
        f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook | Data as of: {get_data_date(rev_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Tax structure: where the money comes from

Two countries can collect the same total in tax yet raise it very differently. This chart breaks each country's tax take into its main sources — taxes on **income and profits**, **social security** contributions, taxes on **goods and services** (consumption, such as sales tax/VAT), **property**, **payroll**, and **other** — each measured as a share of GDP, so the full bar length is the total tax burden and the segments show the mix. Canada's total sits in the middle of the peer group, but its *mix* is distinctive: it leans heavily on income and property taxes and raises comparatively little through social-security contributions and consumption taxes — the reverse of much of continental Europe, where high social-security and value-added taxes dominate. (This is a descriptive comparison; the balance a country strikes between these sources reflects different policy choices, not a better or worse outcome.)

```{python}
#| echo: false
tax_path = "data/fiscal/oecd_tax_structure.csv"
if os.path.exists(tax_path):
    t = pd.read_csv(tax_path)
    TAX_ORDER = ["Income & profits", "Social security", "Goods & services",
                 "Property", "Payroll", "Other"]
    TAX_COLORS = {t: CATEGORICAL_COLORS[i] for i, t in enumerate(TAX_ORDER[:-1])}
    TAX_COLORS["Other"] = CATEGORICAL_OTHER   # residual recedes to the reserved grey
    latest = t.sort_values("year").groupby(["country_code", "tax_type"]).tail(1)
    yr_lo, yr_hi = int(latest["year"].min()), int(latest["year"].max())
    tot = (latest.groupby(["country_code", "country_name"])["pct_gdp"].sum()
           .reset_index().sort_values("pct_gdp"))   # ascending → highest at top
    order = tot["country_name"].tolist()
    piv = latest.pivot_table(index="country_name", columns="tax_type",
                             values="pct_gdp").reindex(order)
    figt = go.Figure()
    for tt in TAX_ORDER:
        figt.add_trace(go.Bar(y=order, x=piv[tt], name=tt, orientation="h",
            marker_color=TAX_COLORS[tt],
            hovertemplate=f"{tt}: %{{x:.1f}}% of GDP<extra>%{{y}}</extra>"))
    # Total at the end of each bar; Canada's label in red so it is easy to find.
    for cn, tv in zip(tot["country_name"], tot["pct_gdp"]):
        is_ca = cn == "Canada"
        figt.add_annotation(x=tv, y=cn, text=f"{tv:.0f}%", xanchor="left", xshift=4,
            showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#d62728" if is_ca else "#777"))
    figt.update_layout(barmode="stack", plot_bgcolor="white", height=600,
        xaxis=dict(title="% of GDP", gridcolor="#e0e0e0", ticksuffix="%"),
        yaxis=dict(title=""),
        legend=dict(orientation="h", yanchor="bottom", y=1.02, x=0),
        margin=dict(l=10, r=40, t=60, b=70))
    figt.add_annotation(
        text=f"Source: OECD Revenue Statistics (general government, latest available {yr_lo}–{yr_hi})",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.11, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))
    figt.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Tax structure data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Government interest costs

Net interest paid by government as a share of GDP — the cost of servicing public debt. Interest is a first charge on revenue: the more that goes to creditors, the less is available for programs or tax relief at a given level of revenue, so lower is less constraining.

```{python}
#| echo: false
int_path = "data/fiscal/oecd_govt_interest.csv"
if os.path.exists(int_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(int_path)
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "govt_interest", title="",
        yaxis_title="% of GDP", show_average=True)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[dict(
        text=f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook (recent years are projections) | Data as of: {get_data_date(int_path)}",
        xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Government interest data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest government interest costs: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(int_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(int_path)
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "govt_interest", "% of GDP",
        f"Source: OECD Economic Outlook | Data as of: {get_data_date(int_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra></extra>")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

A few countries show a *negative* figure: they earn more interest on their financial assets (such as public pension reserves) than they pay on their debt, so net interest is income rather than a cost.

## Defence spending

Beyond the overall balance sheet, how much a country devotes to defence is a distinct spending choice — and the one that draws the most international scrutiny. Military expenditure as a share of GDP is the standard comparison. NATO members have agreed to a guideline of spending at least **2% of GDP** on defence (shown as a dashed line). Israel — not a NATO member, and an outlier at several times the peer norm — is omitted here so the rest of the comparison stays readable.

```{python}
#| echo: false
def_path = "data/fiscal/worldbank_defence.csv"
if os.path.exists(def_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(def_path)
    df = df[df["country_code"] != "ISR"]  # non-NATO outlier; omitting it keeps the scale readable
    fig = peer_comparison_line(df, "year", "defence_pct_gdp", title="",
        yaxis_title="% of GDP", show_average=True)
    fig.add_hline(y=2, line_dash="dash", line_color="#888", opacity=0.6)
    fig.update_layout(annotations=[
        dict(text="NATO minimum target", xref="paper", yref="y", x=0.01,
             xanchor="left", y=2, yshift=9, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#888")),
        dict(text=f"Source: World Bank (data from SIPRI) | Data as of: {get_data_date(def_path)}",
             xref="paper", yref="paper", x=0, y=-0.34, showarrow=False, font=dict(size=10, color="#999"))])
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False, "modeBarButtonsToRemove": ["lasso2d", "select2d"]})
else:
    print("Defence spending data not yet available. Run the data pipeline.")
```

## Latest defence spending: ranked

```{python}
#| echo: false
if os.path.exists(def_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(def_path)
    df = df[df["country_code"] != "ISR"]  # non-NATO outlier; omitting it keeps the scale readable
    fig = ranked_bar(df, "defence_pct_gdp", "% of GDP",
        f"Source: World Bank (data from SIPRI) | Data as of: {get_data_date(def_path)}",
        hover_template="%{y}: %{x:.1f}% of GDP<extra></extra>")
    fig.update_xaxes(range=[0, 5])   # fixed 0–5% scale; keeps the 2% guideline meaningful
    fig.add_vline(x=2, line_dash="dash", line_color="#888", opacity=0.6,
                  annotation_text="2%", annotation_position="top")
    fig.show(config={"displaylogo": False})
```

## Download data

- [Government Gross Debt (CSV)](../data/fiscal/oecd_govt_debt.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [Government Net Debt (CSV)](../data/fiscal/oecd_net_debt.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [Government Budget Balance (CSV)](../data/fiscal/oecd_govt_balance.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [Government Revenue (CSV)](../data/fiscal/oecd_govt_revenue.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [Tax Structure / Revenue Mix (CSV)](../data/fiscal/oecd_tax_structure.csv) — OECD Revenue Statistics
- [Government Interest Costs (CSV)](../data/fiscal/oecd_govt_interest.csv) — OECD Economic Outlook
- [Defence Spending (CSV)](../data/fiscal/worldbank_defence.csv) — World Bank (data from SIPRI)
